scenario_name: Scenario 14 — Anti-Pattern Detection in Existing Architecture
justification: All 6 pass criteria met: (1) All 5 anti-patterns identified by name — shared database, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint, REST-based background jobs, cross-service direct DB reads. (2) Remediation is clearly prioritized across 5 phases with explicit 'why first/second/last' justifications. (3) Shared database marked CRITICAL (Phase 2), direct DB reads marked MEDIUM-HIGH (Phase 3) and grouped with the database work — both treated as highest-risk coupling issues. (4) REST-based jobs explicitly identified as fragile with specific failure modes (overlapping executions, no idempotency, HTTP timeouts killing jobs). (5) Trade-offs for shared DB fix thoroughly covered — loss of cross-service JOINs, event-based consistency, dual-write verification period, 6-week migration timeline. (6) Incremental strangler fig approach emphasized throughout; explicit warnings against big-bang migration.

observations.text: Exceptionally thorough — identified all 5 anti-patterns with detailed severity, blast radius, root cause, and second-order effects for each

Prioritization is well-reasoned: notifications first (highest blast radius, easiest fix), shared DB second (most dangerous but needs planning), reporting parallel with DB work, god endpoint and jobs last
Migration strategies are concrete — strangler fig, dual-write verification, CDC via Debezium, sunset headers with deprecation windows
Trade-offs section includes alternatives considered for each decision, showing genuine architectural reasoning rather than just prescribing solutions
Dependency graph clearly shows which phases can be parallelized, enabling teams to plan capacity
Risks section identifies practical concerns like migration fatigue and consumer resistance, not just technical risks

Notification fan-in rated CRITICAL but placed in Phase 1 as 'easiest to fix' — the rubric expects it at medium-term priority, though the agent's reasoning for doing it first (highest blast radius, lowest effort) is defensible
Output is extremely verbose for a review — the level of detail (domain models, YAML blocks, code examples) goes well beyond what was asked, though quality is high throughout

agent_output_excerpt:
## PR Review — MJ (Architecture)

### Executive Summary

This system exhibits five distinct architectural anti-patterns that compound each other: shared database coupling, synchronous notification fan-in, god endpoint design, HTTP-based job scheduling, and cross-service database reads. Left unaddressed, these create a fragility cascade — any schema migration in Order Service can simultaneously break Courier Service operations AND Reporting Service queries, while a Notification Service outage can
